era_context: The early 11th century marks the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate, the consolidation of feudal Europe under competing kingdoms, the height of the Chola Empire in South India, and the early Viking Age's waning influence. The Fatimid Caliphate controls North Africa and the Levant; the Byzantine Empire is in recovery under the Macedonian Renaissance; the Song Dynasty dominates China. This is a period of religious ferment, with Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism coexisting in complex political arrangements across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Northern Europe beyond Scandinavia and the Frankish sphere
Central Asian steppe kingdoms (sparse chronicle evidence)

events.title: Leif Erikson reaches Vinland (North America)
events.region: North Atlantic; Vinland (modern Newfoundland/northeastern North America)
events.description: Leif Erikson, son of Erik the Red, leads an expedition from Greenland to Vinland, establishing the first documented European settlement in North America. The voyage is recorded in the Norse sagas (Eiríks saga rauða and Grænlendinga saga), which describe a three-part journey: Helluland (Land of Flat Stones), Markland (Land of Forests), and Vinland (Land of Wine/Grapes). Archaeological evidence from L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland confirms Norse presence in North America around this period, though the exact year remains debated. The settlement was short-lived, abandoned within a generation, but represents the first known European contact with the Americas.
key_figures: Erik the Red (father, founder of Greenland settlement)

sources.name: Eiríks saga rauða (Erik the Red's Saga)
Archaeological evidence from L'Anse aux Meadows, Newfoundland

events.certainty_note: The sagas were written 200+ years after the events they describe. L'Anse aux Meadows is confirmed as Norse (c. 1000 CE ±20 years by radiocarbon dating), but the exact year and attribution to Leif Erikson specifically rest on saga testimony. The sagas may conflate multiple voyages or assign credit retrospectively.

events.title: Stephen I crowned King of Hungary
events.description: Stephen I (later Saint Stephen) is crowned the first Christian King of Hungary, consolidating Magyar tribal territories into a feudal Christian kingdom and integrating Hungary into Western Christendom. Stephen had been ruling as a Christian prince since 997 CE; his formal coronation in 1001 (or possibly 1000, sources vary) marks the official Christianization of Hungary and its alignment with Rome rather than Constantinople. He established the bishopric of Esztergom and reorganized the kingdom along feudal lines, creating a stable state that would endure for centuries. This event is foundational to Hungarian national identity and Central European political geography.
key_figures: Stephen I / Saint Stephen (first Christian King of Hungary)

sources.name: Legenda Sancti Stephani (Legend of Saint Stephen)

events.certainty_note: The coronation date is traditionally given as 1001 CE, but some sources place it in 1000 CE. Contemporary papal records confirm Stephen's kingship and Christianization, but the exact coronation date is not pinned to a single source. The event itself is confirmed; the year is approximate.

events.title: Mahmud of Ghazni's raids into northern India begin
events.description: Mahmud of Ghazni, the Turkic ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, launches the first of his seventeen major military campaigns into northern India, targeting Hindu kingdoms and temples for plunder and religious conquest. These raids, which continue until 1027 CE, are among the most destructive military campaigns of the medieval period, resulting in massive loss of life, the destruction of temples, and the displacement of populations. Mahmud's campaigns are celebrated in Islamic sources as holy war (ghazwa) but are recorded in Hindu and Buddhist chronicles as catastrophic invasions. The raids fundamentally altered the religious and political landscape of northern India, weakening Hindu kingdoms and facilitating later Islamic conquest.

sources.name: Al-Utbi, Kitab al-Yamini (Book of the Right Hand)

events.certainty_note: Al-Utbi was a contemporary court historian of Mahmud. The raids are well-documented in both Islamic and Indian sources, though with different interpretive frames. The exact year of the first raid is sometimes given as 1000 CE; 1001 is the more common scholarly dating.

events.title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah orders destruction of Church of the Holy Sepulchre
events.description: The Fatimid Caliph Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah orders the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, one of Christendom's holiest sites. The church is systematically dismantled, and the site is desecrated. This act is recorded in both Islamic and Christian sources, though with different explanations: Islamic sources cite heresy or political rebellion; Christian sources frame it as religious persecution. The destruction of the Holy Sepulchre becomes a major grievance in Christian Europe and is cited as one of the motivations for the First Crusade (launched in 1096 CE). The church is later partially rebuilt under Al-Hakim's successors.
key_figures: Patriarch Sergius II (Christian leader in Jerusalem)

sources.name: Christian pilgrimage accounts and church histories
Archaeological evidence from the Church of the Holy Sepulchre

events.certainty_note: The destruction is attested in multiple sources, but the exact year is debated. Some sources place it in 1009 CE. The event itself is confirmed; the year 1001 is less certain. Modern scholarship tends toward 1009 as the more accurate date.
cross_references: 1096-001 (First Crusade, partly motivated by this destruction)

events.title: Chola Empire under Rajaraja I at its height
events.description: The Chola Empire under Rajaraja I (r. 985–1014 CE) is at its zenith of territorial expansion and cultural achievement. Rajaraja has conquered the Deccan, parts of Sri Lanka, and the Maldives, establishing a maritime trading empire that extends to Southeast Asia and beyond. In 1001 CE, the empire is consolidating its gains and expanding its administrative and cultural institutions. The Brihadeshvara Temple in Thanjavur, commissioned by Rajaraja, is under construction or recently completed, representing the apex of Chola architectural achievement. The Chola navy dominates the Indian Ocean, and the empire's wealth is reflected in extensive temple construction, bronze sculpture, and literary patronage.
key_figures: Rajaraja Chola I's son Rajendra I (future emperor)
events.certainty_note: Rajaraja I's reign dates are well-established through inscriptions. The year 1001 CE falls squarely within his reign, and the empire's status at this time is well-documented through epigraphic evidence.
cross_references: 1014-001 (Rajaraja I's death and Rajendra I's succession)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Leif Erikson's voyage to exactly 1001 CE is traditional but not definitively confirmed by archaeological evidence. L'Anse aux Meadows is dated to c. 1000 CE ±20 years, which could place the settlement in 980–1020 CE. The sagas, written 200+ years later, may have assigned a symbolic date rather than a precise historical one. The destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre is increasingly dated by modern scholarship to 1009 CE rather than 1001 CE, making the inclusion of this event under 1001 questionable. Stephen I's coronation date is disputed between 1000 and 1001 CE in different sources.
historiographic_note: The year 1001 CE is moderately well-documented in European, Islamic, and Indian sources, but with significant regional variation. European sources (Norse sagas, Hungarian chronicles) are often written centuries after the events and may conflate or misdate events. Islamic sources from the Fatimid and Ghaznavid courts are more contemporary but reflect court perspectives and religious biases. Indian sources (temple inscriptions, later chronicles) are epigraphically strong but geographically limited. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and demographic changes are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Central Asia are largely absent from the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: Leif Erikson's voyage to Vinland is followed by his brother Thorvald's expedition
Stephen I's Christianization of Hungary leads to his canonization after his death
Mahmud's raids continue with the attack on Somnath Temple
Destruction of Holy Sepulchre (or 1009 event) becomes a motivation for the First Crusade
Rajaraja I's reign ends; Rajendra I succeeds and continues expansion
